translation: Ha, Meem
commentary:
حمٓ

This surah begins the series of Ha Mim surahs—several consecutive surahs that open with these mysterious letters. Ha Mim serves as the subject of the sentence that follows.

translation: The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Knowing
commentary:
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ

Ha Mim—the revelation of the Book from Allah ﷻ, the Aziz (the Ultimate Authority), the Aleem (the All-Knowing). The grammatical structure tells us that Ha Mim is the Muqtada (subject) and "the revelation of the Book" is the Khabar (predicate). In other words, Ha Mim represents the revelation of the Book from Allah ﷻ, the One whose authority cannot be challenged, the One whose knowledge encompasses everything.

translation: The forgiver of sin, acceptor of repentance, severe in punishment, owner of abundance. There is no deity except Him; to Him is the destination
commentary:
غَافِرِ ٱلذَّنۢبِ وَقَابِلِ ٱلتَّوْبِ شَدِيدِ ٱلْعِقَابِ ذِى ٱلطَّوْلِ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

This ayah presents an unprecedented combination of Allah's names, unique to this surah. Let's examine each divine attribute carefully.

**غَافِرِ ٱلذَّنۢبِ** - The Forgiver of sin. The grammatical form "Ghafir" in Arabic conveys immediacy—someone actively doing something right now. It's like saying "sitting" versus "sitter"—the first indicates the action is happening at this moment. So Ghafir adh-Dhanb means He is forgiving sins as we speak, continuously, at this very moment.

**وَقَابِلِ ٱلتَّوْبِ** - The Accepter of repentance. Notice that the word used here is "Tawb," which is actually the weakest form of the word for repentance in Arabic. The stronger forms are "Tawbah" and "Matab." By using the weakest form, Allah ﷻ is telling us that He accepts even the weakest, most imperfect attempts at repentance. Even your stumbling, incomplete return to Him is acceptable.

**شَدِيدِ ٱلْعِقَابِ** - Intense in bringing about consequences. This attribute balances the mercy mentioned before it, reminding us that Allah's justice is also absolute.

**ذِى ٱلطَّوْلِ** - The Possessor of great might and far-reaching power. The word "Tawl" comes from "Tul," meaning length, suggesting that His power has tremendous reach—extending far beyond what we can imagine.

**لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** - There is no one worthy of worship or obedience except He.

**إِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ** - To Him alone is the final return.

What makes this combination remarkable is how it brings together mercy and justice, forgiveness and consequences, acceptance and accountability—all the dimensions believers need to understand about their relationship with their Creator.

translation: No one disputes concerning the signs of Allah except those who disbelieve, so be not deceived by their [uninhibited] movement throughout the land
commentary:
مَا يُجَـٰدِلُ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ

Nobody debates about the ayat (signs, miracles, verses) of Allah ﷻ except those who have rejected faith. Then their moving around in the lands—their activities, their planning, their meetings, their scheming, their going back and forth—should not deceive you or overwhelm you.

Allah ﷻ is addressing the Messenger ﷺ here, but the lesson extends to all believers. Not only were they debating with him face to face, but there was also significant activity happening behind the scenes. Secret meetings were being held. The chiefs of Makkah were consulting with each other, talking to people from Medina, reaching out to other tribes, plotting and planning. News would trickle back: "I heard they had a meeting today. I heard they're trying to get other groups involved."

The command is clear: don't worry about it. **فَلَا يَغْرُرْكَ تَقَلُّبُهُمْ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ** - whatever secret meetings they're holding shouldn't bother you.

This is a powerful lesson for Muslims who become consumed by conspiracy theories. "They're holding meetings to destroy Islam! They're plotting against us! Did you hear about their secret plans?" Relax. La yaghurruka—don't let it deceive you. The Messenger ﷺ himself was told not to worry about such things. You have your own work to do.

The word مُجَـٰدِلُ refers to face-to-face debate, while تَقَلُّبُ refers to the behind-the-scenes activities, the things you only hear about secondhand. Both should not disturb your focus on your mission.

translation: The people of Noah denied before them and the [disbelieving] factions after them, and every nation intended [a plot] for their messenger to seize him, and they disputed by [using] falsehood to [attempt to] invalidate thereby the truth. So I seized them, and how [terrible] was My penalty
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۖ وَهَمَّتْ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِرَسُولِهِمْ لِيَأْخُذُوهُ ۖ وَجَـٰدَلُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ

Nations before them rejected their messengers—the people of Nuh (AS) and many groups after them. Every single nation aspired to do away with their messenger, wanting to grab hold of him to arrest him. They debated using false proofs to cause the downfall of truth, so I seized them. How then were My consequences?

Here we learn a critical tactic of those who reject faith: **وَجَـٰدَلُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ** - they debated using falsehood. If you're going to engage in debate with someone arguing against the truth, you'd better fact-check what they're saying, because they will make up claims just to win an argument.

**لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ** - so that they can cause the truth to fall. The word "yudhidu" comes from a root that describes an animal collapsing when it's slaughtered. They wanted to see the truth fall publicly, to watch believers stumble in debate, to see them unable to answer their arguments. They used any means necessary, even outright lies, to undermine the truth.

**فَأَخَذْتُهُمْ** - Then I seized them. **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ** - How then were My consequences dealt out? This is a rhetorical question inviting reflection on the historical record of divine justice.

translation: And thus has the word of your Lord come into effect upon those who disbelieved that they are companions of the Fire
commentary:
وَكَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ

That is how the word of your Master—meaning the command, the injunction, the decree of your Master—became a reality against those who disbelieved: that they would become companions of the Fire.

The word "haqqat" means it became manifest, it became real, it was established as truth. The same pattern that played out with previous nations will play out again. This isn't a threat—it's a statement of how divine justice operates.

translation: Those [angels] who carry the Throne and those around it exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and believe in Him and ask forgiveness for those who have believed, [saying], "Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge, so forgive those who have repented and followed Your way and protect them from the punishment of Hellfire
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ ٱلْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَٱغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

Now look at the beautiful continuity of speech. The last thing we learned at the end of Surah Az-Zumar was about angels circling the Throne doing tasbih: **al-hamdu lillahi Rabb al-alamin**. The believers had already entered Jannah, so there was no more istighfar needed for them at that point—only hamd, only praise.

But right now, in this world, those same angels have another job. **ٱلَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ ٱلْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥ** - Those who carry the Throne and those around it—**يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ** - they continue declaring the absolute perfection of Allah ﷻ by praising their Master.

**وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ** - And they continue to believe in Him. Even angels, with all their proximity to Allah ﷻ, are described as believers, reminding us that iman is not just intellectual acknowledgment but ongoing commitment.

**وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟** - And they ask forgiveness for those who have believed. Until believers are finally entered into Jannah, these angels continue making istighfar for them. This is their current responsibility.

Then the angels begin their supplication: **رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا** - Our Master, You encompass all things in mercy and in knowledge.

This acknowledgment is striking. Perhaps the angels have differences of opinion about when certain punishments should occur or other matters of divine wisdom. But whenever they disagree about anything, they return to this foundation: You encompass all things in Your loving mercy and in terms of knowledge—things we know and things we don't know.

Every time angels are mentioned in the Quran making du'a, it's fascinating that knowledge comes up. ** رَبَّنَا مَا خَلَقْتَ هَٰذَا بَاطِلًا سُبْحَانَكَ** - they acknowledge what they don't fully understand. **لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا** - We have no knowledge except what You have taught us.

This is significant because the most important knowledge we have—revelation—is delivered to us by angels. Angels are historical record-keepers. Angels record individual deeds. Angels exist in the closest company to Allah ﷻ. They carry the Throne. They have access to both the unseen world and the seen world. Angel Jibreel (AS) was the teacher of Muhammad ﷺ. So when we discuss knowledge, particularly knowledge of the unseen, angels certainly have an advantage.

But as far as worldly knowledge goes, Allah ﷻ gave humanity an advantage. So much so that when Allah ﷻ asked the angels the names of things, they said, "We don't know. La ilma lana illa ma allamtana—we have no knowledge except what You have taught us." Yet in that more classified, sacred knowledge, they are far above us. And even with their elevated knowledge, they keep referring back to Allah ﷻ being the All-Knowing.

**فَٱغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ سَبِيلَكَ** - Then cover the mistakes of those who made tawbah and followed Your path.

Notice how the two names of Allah ﷻ mentioned at the beginning of this surah—Ghafir adh-Dhanb and Qabil at-Tawb—are now captured together in the angels' prayer: "Faghfir" from one name, "lilladhina taboo" from the other. The angels are combining these divine attributes in their supplication for believers.

**وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ** - And protect them from the punishment of the roaring flame.

translation: Our Lord, and admit them to gardens of perpetual residence which You have promised them and whoever was righteous among their fathers, their spouses and their offspring. Indeed, it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدتَّهُمْ وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

**رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدتَّهُمْ** - Our Master, enter them into the gardens of Eden, those gardens that You promised them.

Then comes a beautiful addition: **وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ** - and whoever was righteous among their fathers, their spouses, and their descendants.

The gardens of Eden—Jannatul Adn—are the highest level of Paradise. But perhaps their fathers were good, they were salih (righteous), but they weren't exactly of the Adn quality. Maybe they earned a lower level of Jannah. So the angels say: If their fathers, spouses or descendants were in a lower level of Jannah, why don't You enter them into Adn too, together with their beloved family members?

This reveals the angels' deep compassion and their understanding of what would complete the believers' joy. They don't just ask for the believers themselves to be elevated—they ask for their righteous family members to be raised to their level so they can be together.

**إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ** - No doubt You are, in fact, truly the One who is the Ultimate Authority and the possessor of all wisdom.

translation: And protect them from the evil consequences [of their deeds]. And he whom You protect from evil consequences that Day - You will have given him mercy. And that is the great attainment
commentary:
وَقِهِمُ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ۚ وَمَن تَقِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

**وَقِهِمُ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ** - And protect them from evil deeds; protect them from all forms of evil.

**وَمَن تَقِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمْتَهُۥ** - And whoever is saved from evil deeds on that Day, then You have absolutely shown mercy to him.

Remember from the previous surah: evil deeds take a physical form on Judgment Day. They become ugly, terrifying manifestations that confront their doers. So whoever is saved from having to see their evil deeds manifested in that way on Judgment Day has received tremendous mercy.

**وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ** - And that, in fact, is the ultimate success.

The angels are defining ultimate success for us: to not have to face our evil deeds on Judgment Day. Not wealth, not status, not worldly achievement—but being spared from confronting the evil we committed. This is what the angels, who understand the reality of the Hereafter far better than we do, describe as the greatest victory.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve will be addressed, "The hatred of Allah for you was [even] greater than your hatred of yourselves [this Day in Hell] when you were invited to faith, but you refused
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنَادَوْنَ لَمَقْتُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ مِن مَّقْتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ

No doubt those who rejected faith are being called out on Judgment Day: **لَمَقْتُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ مِن مَّقْتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ** - The disgust and disappointment of Allah ﷻ with you is far greater than the disgust and disappointment you had with your own selves.

The word مَقْت carries both disgust and disappointment. Remember in the previous surah when they said, "**Hal min shafi'in fayashfa'a lana**—Is there any intercessor who can intercede for us? Can we talk about something else?" They were disgusted with themselves and deeply disappointed.

Now Allah ﷻ responds: The disgust I have with you is way greater than the disappointment and disgust you have with yourselves. And this maqt, this disappointment, it refreshes our memory of **لَا تَقْنَطُوا مِن رَّحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ** - Don't lose hope in Allah's mercy. Don't be disappointed in Allah's mercy.

**إِذْ تُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَتَكْفُرُونَ** - When you were being called continuously to faith, then you continued to be ungrateful and you disbelieved.

translation: They will say, "Our Lord, You made us lifeless twice and gave us life twice, and we have confessed our sins. So is there to an exit any way
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَمَتَّنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَٱعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ

They respond: **رَبَّنَآ أَمَتَّنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا ٱثْنَتَيْنِ** - Our Master, You gave us death twice and You gave us life twice.

This reveals a remarkable reality. We were given a form of death way before we even got here—before we were placed into the wombs of our mothers. Allah ﷻ put us into a state like sleep, a coma of sorts. That's the first death. Then we woke up to consciousness for the first time—that's the first life. Then we experienced death again in this world. And then we'll be given life again on Judgment Day. So that makes two deaths and two lives.

The first experience we had was actually of death before we ever experienced life, because Allah ﷻ gave us death first.

**فَٱعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا** - Now we've come to admit everything, all our sins.

**فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ** - Is there any way out? Any path at all that can lead to an exit? Any way whatsoever?

What are they really asking? They're saying: You did it twice already—how about a third time? You gave me death twice, You gave me life twice. How about another death and another life? Round three? Can we try again? Another chance?

translation: [They will be told], "That is because, when Allah was called upon alone, you disbelieved; but if others were associated with Him, you believed. So the judgement is with Allah, the Most High, the Grand
commentary:
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّهُۥٓ إِذَا دُعِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُۥ كَفَرْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن يُشْرَكْ بِهِۦ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ فَٱلْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْعَلِىِّ ٱلْكَبِيرِ

**ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّهُۥٓ إِذَا دُعِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَحْدَهُۥ كَفَرْتُمْ** - That is your case, because whenever it was the case that Allah ﷻ alone was to be called upon, you were ungrateful, you disbelieved.

**وَإِن يُشْرَكْ بِهِۦ تُؤْمِنُوا۟** - But whenever any associations were made with Him, then you would believe, then you were willing to have faith.

This is the fundamental problem: they rejected pure monotheism but were perfectly comfortable with diluted versions of faith that included other deities alongside Allah ﷻ.

**فَٱلْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْعَلِىِّ ٱلْكَبِيرِ** - Then the ultimate governance and decision lies with Allah ﷻ, the Most High (Al-Ali), the Great (Al-Kabeer).

translation: It is He who shows you His signs and sends down to you from the sky, provision. But none will remember except he who turns back [in repentance]
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقًا ۚ وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ

**هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ** - He is the One who keeps showing you His miraculous signs. The verb is in the continuous tense—He doesn't show them once; He continuously reveals His ayat.

**وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقًا** - And He keeps sending down to you provision from the sky. Even from the sky! Have you ever wondered about that? Rain, from which all provision ultimately derives, comes from the sky.

**وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ** - Yet who makes an effort to remember and reflect? Only the person whose heart keeps turning back to Allah ﷻ. Only the one who repeatedly returns to their Creator.

This is the pattern: Allah ﷻ continuously reveals signs, continuously provides, but only those who actively turn their hearts back to Him actually notice and remember.

translation: So invoke Allah, [being] sincere to Him in religion, although the disbelievers dislike it
commentary:
فَٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

**فَٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ** - Then call on Allah ﷻ, making your deen (your lifestyle, your religion, your entire way of life) sincere to Him.

This combination of words is easy to translate but very heavy to understand. The command isn't just about making sincere du'a. It's about making your entire deen—your lifestyle—sincere before calling on Allah ﷻ.

People pack the masajid on the 27th night of Ramadan. Millions make du'a. But Allah ﷻ is saying: Yes, make du'a to Me, but first purify your lifestyle for Me. Mukhliseena lahu ad-deen—making not just your prayer but your business, your income, your goals, your career, your love and your hate, your aspirations, your future plans—where is the sincerity to Allah ﷻ in all of that?

**وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ** - Even if the disbelievers hate it.

Disbelievers don't necessarily hate Muslims making du'a. But they absolutely hate when our entire life turns to Allah ﷻ, because then we don't do what they want us to do. Then we're not living according to their agenda. That's what they find intolerable.

translation: [He is] the Exalted above [all] degrees, Owner of the Throne; He places the inspiration of His command upon whom He wills of His servants to warn of the Day of Meeting
commentary:
رَفِيعُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتِ ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ يُلْقِى ٱلرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّلَاقِ

**رَفِيعُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتِ** - The Exalted One of all levels, the One possessing the highest rank. This could also mean the One who raises others to high levels. But here "Rafi'" emphasizes that He Himself is elevated in and of Himself. "Marfu'" would mean someone raised Him, but "Rafi'" means He is inherently, essentially elevated above all levels.

**ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ** - The Possessor, the Owner of the Throne. "Dhu" originally means family or close relations, emphasizing the intimacy of ownership.

**يُلْقِى ٱلرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ** - He casts the Ruh (spirit/revelation) from His command onto whoever He wants among His slaves.

There are three scholarly interpretations of "Ruh" here:

1. **Revelation itself** - One of the names of divine revelation is "Ruh" (spirit), as it gives spiritual life.

2. **Angel Jibreel (AS)** - The angel who delivers revelation, who is himself called "Ruh."

3. **The divine decree of life** - The Ruh that is sent down to give life to the deceased, which will happen on Judgment Day.

The most widely held view is that this refers to revelation, particularly given what follows.

**لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّلَاقِ** - So he can warn of the Day of Meeting, the Day of the most powerful meeting. "Talaq" comes from the root meaning to meet, to come together. This is when all of creation will meet Allah ﷻ, when all souls will meet, when all accounts will be settled.

translation: The Day they come forth nothing concerning them will be concealed from Allah. To whom belongs [all] sovereignty this Day? To Allah, the One, the Prevailing
commentary:
يَوْمَ هُم بَـٰرِزُونَ ۖ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَىْءٌ ۚ لِّمَنِ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ

**يَوْمَ هُم بَـٰرِزُون** - The Day when they will be standing face to face, completely exposed, out in the open. "Barizoon" means nothing is hidden, everything is manifest.

**لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَىْءٌ** - Nothing at all stands hidden from Allah ﷻ regarding them. Not a single thing about them is concealed from His knowledge.

**لِّمَنِ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ** - A question is asked on that Day: To whom does the kingdom belong today?

**لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ** - To Allah ﷻ, the One, the Irresistible, the One who cannot be denied, the Supreme Victor over all.

Al-Qahhar means the One whose will cannot be resisted, the One who has ultimate dominion that cannot be challenged or opposed.

translation: This Day every soul will be recompensed for what it earned. No injustice today! Indeed, Allah is swift in account
commentary:
ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۚ لَا ظُلْمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ

**ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ**  - Today every single soul is going to be paid in full for everything that it earned. Every action, every intention, every choice—all of it will be accounted for with perfect precision.

**لَا ظُلْمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ**- There is no injustice today. Not even the smallest particle of injustice will occur.

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ** - Indeed, Allah ﷻ is swift in taking account.

Despite the complexity of every human life, despite the countless deeds of billions of souls, Allah's accounting will be swift. His knowledge encompasses everything instantly, and His judgment will be rendered with perfect speed and perfect accuracy.

This ayah establishes three crucial principles of divine justice: complete accountability for our choices, absolute absence of injustice, and the swiftness of Allah's perfect judgment. These truths should shape how we live today, knowing that every moment, every choice, every action is being recorded and will be answered for on that tremendous Day of Meeting.

translation: And warn them, [O Muhammad], of the Approaching Day, when hearts are at the throats, filled [with distress]. For the wrongdoers there will be no devoted friend and no intercessor [who is] obeyed
commentary:
وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةِ إِذِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ لَدَى ٱلْحَنَاجِرِ كَـٰظِمِينَ ۚ مَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ

The command وَأَنذِرْهُمْ - "And warn them" - directs the Prophet ﷺ to alert people about يَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةِ - "the Day that is drawing near." The word الآزفة comes from a root meaning something that is approaching very rapidly, like when you're about to miss a flight and you have very little time left. It conveys urgency and the certainty of arrival.

The imagery that follows is visceral: إِذِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ لَدَى ٱلْحَنَاجِرِ كَـٰظِمِينَ - "When the hearts are at the throats, suppressed." Picture the moment when the doors for separation are about to close, when the window of time is vanishing rapidly. People are in such terror and anxiety that their hearts feel like they're rising into their throats. The word كَاظِمِين comes from كَظْم, meaning to suppress or swallow down - they're trying to swallow their hearts back down, overwhelmed by fear and anguish.

The ayah then delivers a devastating reality: مَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ - "The wrongdoers will have no intimate friend." The word حَمِيم doesn't just mean any friend - it comes from حُمَّى, meaning fever or warmth. It refers to a warm, close relationship, someone who truly cares. On that Day, no such friend will be there for the oppressors.

وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ - "Nor any intercessor who would be obeyed." Not only will they lack friends, but even if someone attempted to intercede for them, their intercession would carry no weight, no influence. No one's words will be heard or followed on their behalf.

translation: He knows that which deceives the eyes and what the breasts conceal
commentary:
يَعْلَمُ خَآئِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُنِ وَمَا تُخْفِى ٱلصُّدُورُ

This brief but powerful ayah reveals the scope of Allah's knowledge. يَعْلَمُ خَآئِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُن - "He knows the stealing of the eyes" or "the treachery of the glances." Even the smallest movements are captured. When you take a quick look at something you shouldn't, when you glance and then look away before someone notices, when you roll your eyes in disrespect - all of it is known to Him.

Our eyes contain muscles that move so easily, so subtly. We can misuse them in ways that seem invisible to everyone around us. But Allah ﷻ sees every stolen glance, every inappropriate look, every dismissive roll of the eyes. It's not just about looking at forbidden things - it includes the silent disrespect shown through eye movements, like when parents are correcting you and you look down while rolling your eyes.

وَمَا تُخْفِى ٱلصُّدُورُ - "And whatever the hearts conceal." Beyond even the eyes, He knows what lies hidden in the depths of the heart - the thoughts, intentions, feelings and desires that no human can detect. On Judgment Day, even these minute details will be brought forward and held to account.

translation: And Allah judges with truth, while those they invoke besides Him judge not with anything. Indeed, Allah - He is the Hearing, the Seeing
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ يَقْضِى بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَىْءٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ

وَٱللَّهُ يَقْضِى بِٱلْحَقِّ - "And Allah judges with truth" or "passes verdict with justice." His judgment is based on absolute truth and perfect justice. In contrast, وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَىْءٍ - "Those they call upon besides Him do not judge anything at all." The false deities, the idols, the things people worship other than Allah ﷻ - they possess no power to judge, no ability to affect any outcome whatsoever.

The ayah concludes by returning to Allah's perfect attributes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ - "Indeed Allah, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing." He alone possesses complete hearing and complete sight. This connects back to the previous ayah - He hears everything and sees everything, including the theft of the eyes and what hearts conceal.

translation: Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those who were before them? They were greater than them in strength and in impression on the land, but Allah seized them for their sins. And they had not from Allah any protector
commentary:
۞ أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوا۟ هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ

The ayah poses a rhetorical question: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "Have they not traveled through the land?" This is an invitation to observe, to learn from history, to see the evidence that lies all around. فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ - "So they could see how was the outcome of those who came before them."

The lesson is clear: كَانُوا۟ هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "They were greater than them in strength and in the traces they left in the land." When we think of the ruins of ancient civilizations, which nation comes to mind first? The world-famous ruins belong to past nations that were far more powerful, far more advanced in their construction and monuments than those who came after them.

فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ - "So Allah seized them because of their sins." Despite all their power and glory, Allah ﷻ took them to account. وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ - "And they had no protector from Allah" - not a single one who could shield them or offer them any protection from His judgment. The word وَاقٍ comes from the same root as وِقَايَة, meaning protection or defense.

translation: That was because their messengers were coming to them with clear proofs, but they disbelieved, so Allah seized them. Indeed, He is Powerful and severe in punishment
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَكَفَرُوا۟ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ - "That is because" - this explains the reason for their destruction. كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - "Their messengers used to come to them with clear proofs." They weren't left without guidance. Allah ﷻ sent them messengers with undeniable evidence.

فَكَفَرُوا۟ - "But they disbelieved" or "they denied." Despite the clarity of the message and the proofs, they chose rejection. فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ - "So Allah seized them." The same word used in the previous ayah, emphasizing the sudden and complete nature of divine punishment.

إِنَّهُۥ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - "Indeed He is All-Powerful, severe in punishment." The word قَوِىّ emphasizes His absolute might and strength, while شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَاب - "severe in punishment" or "intense in bringing consequences" - shows that when His punishment comes, it is devastating and complete.

translation: And We did certainly send Moses with Our signs and a clear authority
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

Now the Quran transitions to a specific historical example. وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا - "And certainly We sent Musa with Our signs" or "Our miracles." These weren't ordinary signs - they were divine miracles that left no room for doubt.

وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ - "And a clear authority." This refers to the special authority Allah ﷻ granted Musa (AS). In Surah Al-Qasas, Allah ﷻ told Musa (AS) and Harun (AS): "We will give you an authority as a result of which they won't be able to reach you, they won't be able to get to you." This sultan (authority) was a protective power that would keep them safe even in the face of Pharaoh's threats.

translation: To Pharaoh, Haman and Qarun; but they said, "[He is] a magician and a liar
commentary:
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَقَـٰرُونَ فَقَالُوا۟ سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ

إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ وَقَـٰرُونَ - "To Pharaoh and Haman and Qarun." This is significant. Qarun, whom we read about in Surah Al-Qasas, is here grouped together with Pharaoh and Haman because he was working for them, part of their inner circle. They shared the same agenda, the same propaganda, because it all came from the same state apparatus.

فَقَالُوا۟ سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ - "So they said: 'A magician who continually lies.'" The word كَذَّاب in the intensive form indicates perpetual, constant lying - not just an occasional lie, but someone who makes lying their habit. This was their united propaganda against Musa (AS).

translation: And when he brought them the truth from Us, they said, "Kill the sons of those who have believed with him and keep their women alive." But the plan of the disbelievers is not except in error
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَبْنَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ وَٱسْتَحْيُوا۟ نِسَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا كَيْدُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ

فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ مِنْ عِندِنَا - "Then when he brought them the truth from Us" - when the message became undeniable, their response escalated dramatically.

قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَبْنَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ - "They said: 'Kill the sons of those who believed with him.'" This was a new decree. Previously, Pharaoh had ordered the killing of Israelite boys every other year. But now a fresh verdict was passed: anyone who believes in Musa, even if there's just a hint that he believes - kill their children. This was an attempt at genocide to crush the movement before it could spread.

وَٱسْتَحْيُوا۟ نِسَآءَهُمْ - "And let their women live." The same cruel policy continued - humiliate them by leaving the women alive while destroying their future through killing their sons.

وَمَا كَيْدُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ - "But the plot of the disbelievers is nothing but bound to fail" or "is only in ruin." Despite all their elaborate planning, their schemes, their meetings and verdicts - it would all come to nothing. This is also a message to the Prophet ﷺ: don't let the activities of the disbelievers, their gatherings, their sanctions, their plans worry you. Even when they passed a verdict to kill the children of believers, their plan was destined to fail.

translation: And Pharaoh said, "Let me kill Moses and let him call upon his Lord. Indeed, I fear that he will change your religion or that he will cause corruption in the land
commentary:
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ذَرُونِىٓ أَقْتُلْ مُوسَىٰ وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُۥٓ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ أَوْ أَن يُظْهِرَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْفَسَادَ

To understand this ayah, we need some historical context. According to some accounts, "Pharaoh" was a title, not a personal name - like Ramses I, Ramses II, and so on. The Pharaoh at the time when baby Musa (AS) was found and raised in the palace was the older king. By the time Musa (AS) grew up, left Egypt, spent eight to ten years away, went to the mountain and received revelation, and then returned - that older king had retired and passed governance to his son.

This younger Pharaoh had been raised almost as a younger brother to Musa (AS) in his childhood - Ramses II, if we follow these historical accounts. They had a close relationship. This explains why, when Musa (AS) returned and challenged Pharaoh's authority, this younger Pharaoh couldn't immediately order his execution. Allah ﷻ says in the Quran: "We had cast upon you a special love from Me" - even Pharaoh had been unable to simply kill him.

The suggestion to kill Musa (AS) actually came from Pharaoh's chiefs and generals, not from Pharaoh himself initially. They had never liked Musa (AS), and when he accidentally killed an Egyptian, they were already plotting against him. But Pharaoh wasn't ready to give that order. As time went on and Musa's message gained traction, the chiefs kept pressuring Pharaoh: "You're looking soft on national security. We can't trust you with national threats because you seem soft on the enemy."

Eventually, to display his strength and prove himself to his generals, Pharaoh had to say: ذَرُونِىٓ أَقْتُلْ مُوسَىٰ - "Let me kill Musa." The word ذَرُونِي is fascinating. Though it's a command form, it doesn't actually mean "give me permission." 

Think of this scene: You're playing basketball and someone fouls you hard. You fall to the ground, upset, about to fight, and your friends hold you back. You say "Let me go! Let me at him!" You're not asking permission - you're telling them to stand back because you'll handle it yourself. That's exactly what Pharaoh is doing here. He's telling his generals: "You don't need to take care of him - I'll kill Musa myself." This way, they would think, "He's the man! We thought he was soft, but he's going to do it himself."

وَلْيَدْعُ رَبَّهُۥٓ - "And let him call upon his Lord." Pharaoh is so arrogant he's essentially saying, "Let him pray to his God - it won't help him."

إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُبَدِّلَ دِينَكُمْ - "I fear he might change your religion." Pharaoh frames this as concern for national unity: "I'm seeing differences even among some of you generals, and I don't like it."

أَوْ أَن يُظْهِرَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْفَسَادَ - "Or that he will cause corruption to appear in the land." In other words: "People will start rebelling against us. We can't have that. I have to kill him myself and be done with him." This is how tyrants frame their oppression - as maintaining order and preventing chaos.

translation: But Moses said, "Indeed, I have sought refuge in my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Account
commentary:
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم مِّن كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ

The news of Pharaoh's kill order reached Musa (AS). His response was not to mobilize an army or devise a counter-strategy, but to turn to Allah : وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُم - "And Musa said: 'Indeed I seek refuge with my Master and your Master.'"

Notice he says "my Master and YOUR Master" - even addressing those who want to kill him, he reminds them that they share the same true Lord, whether they acknowledge it or not.

مِّن كُلِّ مُتَكَبِّرٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ - "From every arrogant tyrant who does not believe in the Day of Accounting." Musa (AS) identifies the core problem: arrogance combined with disbelief in accountability. When someone doesn't believe they'll be held to account, their arrogance knows no bounds. The only protection against such a person is Allah ﷻ Himself.

translation: And a believing man from the family of Pharaoh who concealed his faith said, "Do you kill a man [merely] because he says, 'My Lord is Allah' while he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord? And if he should be lying, then upon him is [the consequence of] his lie; but if he should be truthful, there will strike you some of what he promises you. Indeed, Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar
commentary:
وَقَالَ رَجُلٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَكْتُمُ إِيمَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَتَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلًا أَن يَقُولَ رَبِّىَ ٱللَّهُ وَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَكُ كَـٰذِبًا فَعَلَيْهِ كَذِبُهُۥ ۖ وَإِن يَكُ صَادِقًا يُصِبْكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى يَعِدُكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ

Up until this moment, one man had remained silent. During all the debates, when Musa (AS) first confronted Pharaoh, when the public event with the magicians took place and they were martyred - through all of this, he stayed quiet. But now that the kill order has been issued, he can no longer remain silent.

وَقَالَ رَجُلٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ - "And a believing man said." Why mention رَجُل (rajul) when it's already clear it's a man? The word comes from رِجْل- foot - implying someone who stands firm, who holds his ground. A real man, in the truest sense. This wasn't just any believer - this was someone who would stand up for what's right regardless of the consequences.

مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ - "From the family of Pharaoh" or "from among Pharaoh's people" - someone from the inner circle, from the court itself. يَكْتُمُ إِيمَـٰنَهُۥٓ - "Who had been concealing his faith." All this time, he had hidden his belief, waiting for the right moment.

أَتَقْتُلُونَ رَجُلًا أَن يَقُولَ رَبِّىَ ٱللَّهُ - "Are you going to kill a man simply because he says 'My Lord is Allah'?" This is a powerful rhetorical question that cuts to the heart of the injustice.

وَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ - "And he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord." The believer reminds them of something they already know: their own chiefs had gone to Musa (AS) begging him to ask his Lord to remove the punishments. They had acknowledged his power, his connection to the divine.

Then he presents them with a logical argument: وَإِن يَكُ كَـٰذِبًا فَعَلَيْهِ كَذِبُهُۥ - "If he is a liar, then his lie will only harm him." A simple lie can only hurt the liar himself.

وَإِن يَكُ صَادِقًا يُصِبْكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى يَعِدُكُمْ- "But if he is truthful, then some of what he is promising you will strike you." The believer is saying: "If there's even the slightest possibility in your minds that he might be telling the truth, then you should be terrified of what he's warning you about."

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ- "Indeed Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar." Here's the profound subtext: the believer is saying that Musa (AS) has never gone beyond his limits, and he's not known as a liar. But Pharaoh? Pharaoh is known for both excess and lying. So this statement, while appearing neutral, is actually a declaration that Allah ﷻ is not with Pharaoh.

This tells us something crucial: when the second encounter happened with the magicians, when they declared their faith and were martyred before the entire public, it didn't just affect common people. It rattled some of the generals and officials too, including this believing man - to the point where he now speaks out against the kill order issued directly by Pharaoh, in front of him and all the other generals. He's essentially calling for a coup, asking them to rise up against their leader.

translation: O my people, sovereignty is yours today, [your being] dominant in the land. But who would protect us from the punishment of Allah if it came to us?" Pharaoh said, "I do not show you except what I see, and I do not guide you except to the way of right conduct
commentary:
يَـٰقَوْمِ لَكُمُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ظَـٰهِرِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَمَن يَنصُرُنَا مِنۢ بَأْسِ ٱللَّهِ إِن جَآءَنَا ۚ قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ مَآ أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَرَىٰ وَمَآ أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ

The believer continues his speech: يَـٰقَوْمِ لَكُمُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ظَـٰهِرِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "O my people, you have the kingdom today, dominant in the land." He acknowledges their current power and position.

فَمَن يَنصُرُنَا مِنۢ بَأْسِ ٱللَّهِ إِن جَآءَنَا - "But who will help us against the punishment of Allah if it comes to us?" This is the critical question: all your earthly power means nothing if divine punishment arrives. Who will protect you then?

Now Pharaoh has to respond, and notice his tone - it's completely changed. He doesn't respond with his usual arrogance. He doesn't say, "I am your supreme lord!" or threaten to cut people up like he did with the magicians. Instead: قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ مَآ أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَرَىٰ - "Pharaoh said: 'I am only showing you what I see.'" He takes a defensive stance, as if to say, "Look, I'm just telling you what I think. That's all."

وَمَآ أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ - "And I am only guiding you to the path of right conduct." He's trying to sound reasonable, measured - not the tyrant but the concerned leader.

This shows us something remarkable: a debate is now happening inside the parliament itself. Pharaoh is afraid. He needs to convince his generals because he no longer has the absolute authority he once had. Those days of unquestioned power are finished. He can't just say "take him away" anymore - he has to persuade, to argue, to maintain the appearance of reasonableness.

translation: And he who believed said, "O my people, indeed I fear for you [a fate] like the day of the companies
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُم مِّثْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ

The believer, emboldened, continues his speech: وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ - "And the one who believed said." He's no longer described as concealing his faith - now he's openly "the one who believed."

يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُم مِّثْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ - "O my people, I fear for you the like of the day of the confederates" - referring to the various groups and nations that were destroyed before them.

translation: Like the custom of the people of Noah and of 'Aad and Thamud and those after them. And Allah wants no injustice for [His] servants
commentary:
مِثْلَ دَأْبِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۚ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعِبَادِ

مِثْلَ دَأْبِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ - "Like the fate of the people of Nuh and 'Ad and Thamud." This is fascinating because we're now learning that Musa (AS) taught his followers about these previous nations and their prophets - Nuh (AS), Hud (AS) who was sent to 'Ad, and Salih (AS) who was sent to Thamud.

وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ - "And those who came after them." The list goes on - there were many more examples of divine punishment throughout history.

وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعِبَادِ - "And Allah does not intend any injustice for His slaves." The believer emphasizes that Allah ﷻ is not unjust - He gives warnings, sends messengers, provides clear proofs. Destruction only comes after people have been thoroughly warned and have chosen to reject the truth.

translation: And O my people, indeed I fear for you the Day of Calling
commentary:
وَيَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّنَادِ

The believer shifts from historical examples to the Hereafter: وَيَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّنَادِ - "And O my people, I fear for you the Day of Calling Out" or "the Day of Loud Crying."

The word التَّنَادِ comes from نِدَاء, meaning to call out. But this intense form suggests loud, desperate calling - people crying out to each other, screaming, wailing. It's a day when panic and chaos reign, when people will be frantically calling for help that won't come.

translation: The Day you will turn your backs fleeing; there is not for you from Allah any protector. And whoever Allah leaves astray - there is not for him any guide
commentary:
يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ مَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ

يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ مُدْبِرِينَ - "The Day when you will turn back fleeing." Picture people trying to run away, turning their backs, desperately trying to escape.

مَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ عَاصِمٍ - "You will have no protector from Allah." When His decree comes, there's nowhere to run, no one who can shield you.

وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ - "And whoever Allah allows to go astray, there will be no guide for him whatsoever." If Allah ﷻ leaves someone in their misguidance, no power in the universe can guide them.

translation: And Joseph had already come to you before with clear proofs, but you remained in doubt of that which he brought to you, until when he died, you said, 'Never will Allah send a messenger after him.' Thus does Allah leave astray he who is a transgressor and skeptic
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ يُوسُفُ مِن قَبْلُ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا زِلْتُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا جَآءَكُم بِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا هَلَكَ قُلْتُمْ لَن يَبْعَثَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ رَسُولًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ

This ayah reveals something extraordinary: وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ يُوسُفُ مِن قَبْلُ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - "And certainly Yusuf came to you before with clear proofs."

This is the people of Egypt being reminded. Yusuf (AS) had become a minister, had authority in Egypt. We knew he had a kingdom, but now we're learning something more: when he was in that position of authority, he preached Islam. He tried to call the Egyptian people to the worship of Allah ﷻ alone.

فَمَا زِلْتُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا جَآءَكُم بِهِۦ - "But you remained in doubt about what he brought to you." Despite the clarity of his message and proofs, they were skeptical.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا هَلَكَ قُلْتُمْ لَن يَبْعَثَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ رَسُولًا - "Until when he died, you said, 'Allah will never send a messenger after him.'"

The word هَلَكَ is interesting. While مَاتَ is the general word for dying, هَلَكَ is typically used for a violent or dramatic death. The Quran is ambiguous here about exactly what happened to Yusuf (AS), but the word choice may suggest something other than a peaceful passing.

Their response was to say that Yusuf's message was unique, that his talk of one God and the Hereafter was strange, and that surely no one would come after him with such teachings again. They dismissed it as an anomaly.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ - "That is how Allah leaves astray whoever transgresses limits and continues to doubt."

What's remarkable here is that through the mouth of this believer, Allah ﷻ has drawn a parallel between the legacy of Musa (AS) and the legacy of Yusuf (AS). There's a connection being made between these two prophets and their experiences in Egypt. Both brought clear proofs, both faced doubt and rejection, and now both stories serve as warnings.

translation: Those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without an authority having come to them - great is hatred [of them] in the sight of Allah and in the sight of those who have believed. Thus does Allah seal over every heart [belonging to] an arrogant tyrant
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۖ كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ

ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ - "Those who argue about the signs of Allah without any authority that has come to them." They debate against divine revelation and clear proofs without any legitimate basis, without any real evidence or authority to support their position.

كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - "How extremely hateful it is before Allah and before those who believe!" The word مَقْت indicates intense disgust and hatred. Their baseless arguments against truth are utterly despicable both to Allah ﷻ and to the believers.

كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ جَبَّارٍ - "Thus does Allah seal the heart of every arrogant tyrant." The word يَطْبَعُ  means to seal, stamp, or imprint. When someone persists in arrogance and tyranny, making baseless arguments against truth, Allah ﷻ eventually seals their heart. The vicious arguments and disgusting counters become imprinted on their hearts, and they lose the ability to recognize truth.

Notice the two qualities mentioned: مُتَكَبِّر - arrogant, one who sees himself as above others and above the truth; and جَبَّار - a tyrant, one who forces his will on others. These two traits together create an impenetrable barrier against guidance.

translation: And Pharaoh said, "O Haman, construct for me a tower that I might reach the ways
commentary:
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ ٱبْنِ لِى صَرْحًا لَّعَلِّىٓ أَبْلُغُ ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبَ

After this powerful speech from the believer, Pharaoh wants to ridicule Musa's religion and divert attention from the serious arguments just made. So he turns to Haman: وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ يَـٰهَـٰمَـٰنُ ٱبْنِ لِى صَرْحًا - "And Pharaoh said: 'O Haman, build for me a tower.'"

The word صَرْح means a decorated, beautiful, impressive tower - something that displays power and wealth. - "So that I may reach the means" or "the ways" - one level after another, climbing higher and higher.

translation: The ways into the heavens - so that I may look at the deity of Moses; but indeed, I think he is a liar." And thus was made attractive to Pharaoh the evil of his deed, and he was averted from the [right] way. And the plan of Pharaoh was not except in ruin
commentary:
أَسْبَـٰبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ كَـٰذِبًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَصُدَّ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۚ وَمَا كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ إِلَّا فِى تَبَابٍ

أَسْبَـٰبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ - "The means to the heavens" or "the layers of the sky." فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِ مُوسَىٰ - "So I can climb up and discover for myself and reach the God of Musa."

This is dripping with mockery and sarcasm. Why does he want to go up high to talk to God? Because that's what Musa (AS) did - he went up Mount Sinai to speak with Allah ﷻ. Pharaoh is making fun of this, as if to say, "If God is up there, I'll just build a tall tower and go talk to Him myself."

وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّهُۥ كَـٰذِبًا - "And I certainly think him to be a liar." He's publicly declaring his disbelief and mocking the entire concept of a God beyond his reach.

But then Allah ﷻ reveals the reality: وَكَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ - "And thus was beautified for Pharaoh the evil of his deed" or "his ugliest actions were made to appear beautiful to him." When someone persists in denial and arrogance, their worst deeds start to look good to them. They lose the ability to see their own corruption.

وَصُدَّ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - "And he was averted from the path" or "blocked from the way." The passive voice here is significant - he was turned away, prevented from finding guidance. After all his arrogance and mockery, the door to truth was closed to him.

وَمَا كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ إِلَّا فِى تَبَابٍ - "And the plot of Pharaoh was not except in ruin." The word تَبَاب means complete loss, utter ruin, total destruction. All his schemes, his tower-building mockery, his attempts to discredit Musa (AS) - all of it was destined for failure and ruin.

This is the ultimate message: no matter how powerful the tyrant, no matter how elaborate his plans, no matter how much he mocks the truth - if he sets himself against Allah ﷻ, his end is guaranteed destruction. The believer spoke truth to power and survived to tell the tale, while Pharaoh and all his might ended up at the bottom of the sea.

translation: And he who believed said, "O my people, follow me, I will guide you to the way of right conduct
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ

The believing man now steps forward with remarkable courage. يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ - "O my people! Follow me; I will guide you to the path of right conduct." Notice the direct opposition to Fir'awn. Where Fir'awn claimed to have the path, this believer says, "No, سَبِيلَ ٱلرَّشَادِ (the path of uprightness) is with me."

This is significant because it shows that a single believer, armed with truth, can stand against the most powerful tyrant in his society. He doesn't claim authority from his position or credentials - he's simply pointing to what he has learned from Musa (AS).

translation: O my people, this worldly life is only [temporary] enjoyment, and indeed, the Hereafter - that is the home of [permanent] settlement
commentary:
يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَتَـٰعٌ وَإِنَّ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ هِىَ دَارُ ٱلْقَرَارِ

The believer begins his speech by addressing the fundamental delusion of his people: يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَتَـٰعٌ - "O my people! This worldly life is nothing but temporary enjoyment." The word مَتَـٰعٌ refers to utilities, temporary benefits that serve a purpose but don't last.

وَإِنَّ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ هِىَ دَارُ ٱلْقَرَارِ - "And indeed, the Hereafter - that is the home of settlement." The word قَرَارِ means a place where you settle down, where you find rest and permanence. This world is just a temporary station; the real home, the place where you'll finally be able to relax and settle, is the afterlife.

translation: Whoever does an evil deed will not be recompensed except by the like thereof; but whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer - those will enter Paradise, being given provision therein without account
commentary:
مَنْ عَمِلَ سَيِّئَةً فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

This ayah presents a beautiful contrast in divine justice. مَنْ عَمِلَ سَيِّئَةً فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا - "Whoever does an evil deed will not be recompensed except the like thereof." Notice the precision: evil is matched exactly, not exceeded. Allah's ﷻ justice ensures no one is punished beyond what they deserve.

But look at what happens with good deeds: وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ - "But whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while being a believer." The explicit mention of both male and female emphasizes the equality of spiritual opportunity and reward before Allah ﷻ.

فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ - "Those will enter Paradise, being given provision therein without account." The reward isn't just equal - it's limitless. While evil is matched precisely, good is multiplied beyond measure. This is the mercy and generosity of Allah ﷻ.

A remarkable aspect of this passage is the depth of knowledge this believing man demonstrates. He speaks about Jannah, about previous nations, about the precise compensation for deeds. How does he know all this? He's been listening carefully to Musa (AS). This teaches us something essential: you don't need decades of Islamic education before you can speak truth. This man is a new Muslim who has simply absorbed the messages he's heard, and he's ready to speak up.

This challenges the common excuse: "I don't have enough knowledge to say anything." Yes, complex scholarly discussions require expertise, but the fundamental truths of Islam - the oneness of Allah ﷻ, the reality of the afterlife, the call to righteousness - these are clear enough that anyone who understands them has an obligation to speak them. The magicians who believed in Musa (AS) immediately spoke up. The man who ran from the far end of the city in Surah Ya Sin gave his testimony. Knowledge of the truth creates responsibility to share it.

translation: And O my people, how is it that I invite you to salvation while you invite me to the Fire
commentary:
۞ وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَا لِىٓ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّجَوٰةِ وَتَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ

The believer expresses genuine bewilderment at the inversion of values around him: وَيَـٰقَوْمِ مَا لِىٓ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلنَّجَوٰةِ وَتَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ - "And O my people! Why is it that I invite you to salvation while you invite me to the Fire?"

This reveals that his former colleagues and friends had been pressuring him. Perhaps other generals came to him saying, "What's wrong with you? Hold on to your religion! Aren't you a patriot anymore? You're hurting the nation!" They saw his conversion as betrayal, as madness. But he sees their position clearly: they're calling him to the Fire while he's calling them to rescue.

translation: You invite me to disbelieve in Allah and associate with Him that of which I have no knowledge, and I invite you to the Exalted in Might, the Perpetual Forgiver
commentary:
تَدْعُونَنِى لِأَكْفُرَ بِٱللَّهِ وَأُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ وَأَنَا۠ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرِ

He clarifies what their call actually means: تَدْعُونَنِى لِأَكْفُرَ بِٱللَّهِ وَأُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ - "You invite me to disbelieve in Allah and associate with Him that of which I have no knowledge." This phrase "of which I have no knowledge" is powerful. He's saying their religion has no foundation in reality, no basis in truth. It's pure fabrication.

وَأَنَا۠ أَدْعُوكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرِ - "While I invite you to the Exalted in Might, the Perpetual Forgiver." Notice the choice of divine names. Not Fir'awn who claims power - but Allah ﷻ, the truly Mighty (العَزِيز). And unlike Fir'awn who shows no mercy, Allah ﷻ is الغَفَّار - the One who forgives continuously, perpetually. These names directly counter everything Fir'awn represents.

translation: Assuredly, that to which you invite me has no [response to a] supplication in this world or in the Hereafter; and indeed, our return is to Allah, and indeed, the transgressors will be companions of the Fire
commentary:
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّمَا تَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَعْوَةٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَلَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ مَرَدَّنَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَأَنَّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ

لَا جَرَمَ means "no doubt" or even carries the force of an oath - "I swear to God." أَنَّمَا تَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَعْوَةٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَلَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - "What you invite me to has no basis for a call in this world or the Hereafter." It's fluff, it's spin, it's propaganda with nothing behind it.

وَأَنَّ مَرَدَّنَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - "And our return is to Allah." This is the reality they're running from. No matter how much power Fir'awn wields now, everyone's ultimate return is to Allah ﷻ.

وَأَنَّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ - "And indeed, the transgressors are the companions of the Fire." The word مُسْرِفِين refers to those who go beyond the limits set for them, who exceed all boundaries in their rebellion.

translation: And you will remember what I [now] say to you, and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed, Allah is Seeing of [His] servants
commentary:
فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ مَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ

فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ مَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ - "Soon you will remember what I am telling you." This isn't a threat; it's a statement of inevitable reality. The day will come when they'll recall his words and realize he was right.

وَأُفَوِّضُ أَمْرِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - "And I entrust my affair to Allah." The word أُفَوِّضُ means to completely hand over something to someone else's control. He's reached the limit of what he can do. He's delivered the message, made his case, spoken the truth. Now he leaves the outcome to Allah ﷻ.

This suggests a tragic possibility: that they may have executed him for treason. When a believer speaks truth to power and can do no more, all that remains is to say, "I leave my matter to Allah ﷻ." إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ - "Indeed, Allah is Seeing of His servants." Even if no one else witnesses what happens to this believer, Allah ﷻ sees everything.

translation: So Allah protected him from the evils they plotted, and the people of Pharaoh were enveloped by the worst of punishment
commentary:
فَوَقَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِ مَا مَكَرُوا۟ ۖ وَحَاقَ بِـَٔالِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ

فَوَقَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِ مَا مَكَرُوا۟ - "So Allah protected him from the evils of what they plotted." There's a difference of opinion among scholars about who was protected. Some say the believing man himself was saved from their plots - perhaps they became so busy debating him that Musa (AS) had time to escape, and this believer served as a shield. Others suggest that even if he was killed, Allah ﷻ protected him from the spiritual harm they intended, securing his place in Paradise.

وَحَاقَ بِـَٔالِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ - "And there came upon the people of Fir'awn the worst of punishment." The word حَاقَ means to be completely surrounded, captured, enveloped. The punishment they intended for others became a trap that closed around them.

translation: The Fire, they are exposed to it morning and evening. And the Day the Hour appears [it will be said], "Make the people of Pharaoh enter the severest punishment
commentary:
ٱلنَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ ٱلْعَذَابِ

This ayah provides crucial evidence for punishment in the grave: ٱلنَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا - "The Fire - they are exposed to it morning and evening." This is happening now, in the life of the grave. The terms "morning and evening" indicate the passage of worldly time.

وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ ٱلْعَذَابِ - "And the Day the Hour appears [it will be said], 'Make the people of Fir'awn enter the severest punishment.'" This is different from the first punishment. On the Day of Judgment, there is no morning and evening - time as we know it has ended. So they experience the Fire now in their graves, and then on the Day of Resurrection, they'll be entered into the worst punishment.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us that in the grave, either a window to Paradise opens and its breeze comes through, or a window to the Fire opens and its heat reaches the deceased. This ayah makes that reality irrefutable.

translation: And [mention] when they will argue within the Fire, and the weak will say to those who had been arrogant, "Indeed, we were [only] your followers, so will you relieve us of a share of the Fire
commentary:
وَإِذْ يَتَحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ فَيَقُولُ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ

وَإِذْ يَتَحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ - "And when they will dispute within the Fire." The word يَتَحَآجُّونَ indicates making cases against each other, arguing back and forth.

فَيَقُولُ ٱلضُّعَفَـٰٓؤُا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ - "The weak will say to those who were arrogant." In this life, they were followers; in the next, they're looking for someone to blame.

إِنَّا كُنَّا لَكُمْ تَبَعًا فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّغْنُونَ عَنَّا نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ - "Indeed, we were your followers, so will you relieve us of a share of the Fire?" They're asking: "Can you use those connections you had? Can you pull some strings to get us out of at least some portion of the flames?"

translation: Those who had been arrogant will say, "Indeed, all [of us] are in it. Indeed, Allah has judged between the servants
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيهَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ ٱلْعِبَادِ

The response from the arrogant is devastating in its simplicity: قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيهَآ - "Those who were arrogant will say, 'Indeed, all of us are in it.'" We're all the same now. We're all inmates together.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ ٱلْعِبَادِ - "Indeed, Allah has judged between the servants." The verdict has been passed. There's no appeal, no connections to pull, no favors to call in. The leaders who seemed so powerful in the world are just as helpless as everyone else in the Fire.

translation: And those in the Fire will say to the keepers of Hell, "Supplicate your Lord to lighten for us a day from the punishment
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْمًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ

Having given up hope on their worldly leaders, they turn to the angels: وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ - "And those in the Fire will say to the keepers of Hell."

ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْمًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ - "Call upon your Lord to lighten for us a day from the punishment." Notice they can't even ask Allah ﷻ directly - they know He won't listen to them. So they're asking the angels: "Can you at least get us one light day? Just one day off from the punishment? We're not asking for much - just a weekend in Hell where the temperature's turned down a little."

translation: They will say, "Did there not come to you your messengers with clear proofs?" They will say, "Yes." They will reply, "Then supplicate [yourselves], but the supplication of the disbelievers is not except in error
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أَوَلَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۚ قَالُوا۟ فَٱدْعُوا۟ ۗ وَمَا دُعَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ

The angels respond with a question: قَالُوٓا۟ أَوَلَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - "They will say, 'Did there not come to you your messengers with clear proofs?'"

قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ - "They will say, 'Yes.'" They can't deny it. The messengers came, the evidence was clear.

قَالُوا۟ فَٱدْعُوا۟ - "They will respond, 'Then you call.'" The angels essentially say: "You make the du'a yourselves." They won't intercede for those who rejected the messengers.

وَمَا دُعَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ - "And the supplication of the disbelievers is not but in error." Their prayers fall into a void. They go nowhere. When you had the chance to call on Allah ﷻ, you refused. Now your calls are lost in delusion.

translation: Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand
commentary:
إِنَّا لَنَنصُرُ رُسُلَنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ

After showing us the fate of those who rejected the messengers, Allah ﷻ makes a powerful promise: إِنَّا لَنَنصُرُ رُسُلَنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - "Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world."

This support doesn't always look like worldly victory. Sometimes the support is protection, guidance, or the ultimate victory of truth even after apparent defeat. The believing man from Fir'awn's family was supported - whether through physical protection or eternal reward.

وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ - "And on the Day when the witnesses will stand." The word ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ is the plural of both "witnesses" and "testimonies." It's beautiful that the plural is used with a singular verb (يَقُومُ), suggesting that on that Day, both the witnesses and their testimonies will stand upright together in perfect truth.

translation: The Day their excuse will not benefit the wrongdoers, and they will have the curse, and they will have the worst home
commentary:
يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ ۖ وَلَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ

يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ - "The Day their excuse will not benefit the wrongdoers." All the rationalizations, all the justifications they offered in this life, will be worthless.

وَلَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ - "And they will have the curse." The curse refers to being cast far away from Allah's ﷻ mercy.

وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ - "And they will have the worst home." The worst possible residence for themselves, in stark contrast to the believers' eternal home in Paradise.

translation: And We had certainly given Moses guidance, and We caused the Children of Israel to inherit the Scripture
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَأَوْرَثْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ

Allah ﷻ now reminds us of the historical reality: وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ - "And We had certainly given Musa guidance." Musa (AS) had been given the ultimate guidance from Allah ﷻ.

وَأَوْرَثْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ - "And We caused the Children of Israel to inherit the Scripture." The word أَوْرَثْنَا (awrathna) means to make someone inherit something, to pass it down as a legacy.

translation: As guidance and a reminder for those of understanding
commentary:
هُدًى وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

هُدًى وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ - "As guidance and a reminder for those of understanding."

This isn't just historical information. Allah ﷻ is teaching us about the role of the Quran. Just as the Scripture was meant to guide and remind the Children of Israel, the Quran is meant to guide us, counsel us, advise us, and serve as a powerful reminder for those who possess sound minds (أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ).

translation: So be patient, [O Muhammad]. Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth. And ask forgiveness for your sin and exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord in the evening and the morning
commentary:
فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ

After recounting the story of Musa (AS) and the believer who stood against Fir'awn, Allah ﷻ directly addresses the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ - "So be patient. Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth."

وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ - "And ask forgiveness for your sin." This requires careful understanding. The Prophet ﷺ has such high standards for himself that what he considers a shortcoming would be our greatest achievement. The word ذَنۢب (dhanb) can refer to something embarrassing or something whose consequences are regrettable, even if the action itself wasn't wrong. Sometimes the Prophet ﷺ felt he could have done better in delivering the message, and this sense of responsibility led him to seek forgiveness, even though he was faultless.

This reveals something beautiful about decent versus indecent people. When things don't go as expected, decent people examine themselves first. They ask, "What could I have done better?" This becomes an opportunity to raise their ranks before Allah ﷻ. Indecent people, on the other hand, blame everything else - the weather, the people, the society, the times - anything but themselves.

The Prophet ﷺ represents the highest level of decency. Despite being in the most public position imaginable, where it's hardest to accept blame, he takes complete responsibility. He sees shortcomings where no one else can see them and seeks forgiveness for them. This is the greatness of our Messenger ﷺ.

وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ - "And exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord in the evening and the morning." At all times of day - late afternoon (العَشِي) and early morning (الإِبْكَار) - continue to declare the perfection of your Master. When the Prophet ﷺ felt inadequate despite his tremendous efforts, the solution was to turn to Allah ﷻ in istigfar and tasbih.

translation: Indeed, those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without [any] authority having come to them - there is not within their breasts except pride, [the extent of] which they cannot reach. So seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, it is He who is the Hearing, the Seeing
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ ۙ إِن فِى صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ مَّا هُم بِبَـٰلِغِيهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ

Now Allah ﷻ contrasts the humble Prophet ﷺ with those filled with arrogance: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ سُلْطَـٰنٍ أَتَىٰهُمْ - "Indeed, those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without any authority having come to them."

إِن فِى صُدُورِهِمْ إِلَّا كِبْرٌ - "There is not in their breasts except pride." They have nothing but arrogance in their hearts. The Prophet ﷺ, who hasn't even made a mistake but thinks he might have, is told to seek forgiveness and declare Allah's ﷻ greatness. Meanwhile, these people are so full of themselves they can't see any greatness beyond their own egos. All that space in their hearts that should be filled with humility before Allah ﷻ is occupied by their self-absorbed attitude.

مَّا هُم بِبَـٰلِغِيهِ - "They will not reach it." They're not going to be able to overcome it, pull it out, or reach what they're aiming for. The arrogance buried deep in their hearts will be their downfall.

فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ - "So seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, it is He who is the Hearing, the Seeing." The protection against arrogance is to continuously seek Allah's ﷻ refuge.

translation: The creation of the heavens and earth is greater than the creation of mankind, but most of the people do not know
commentary:
لَخَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

To those filled with كِبْر (arrogance, pride in being big), Allah ﷻ says: لَخَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ ٱلنَّاسِ - "The creation of the heavens and earth is greater than the creation of mankind." Notice the wordplay: you think you're كَبِير (big)? Let Me tell you what's أَكْبَر (greater).

We understand this reality more than any generation before us. We can watch documentaries showing the depths of the universe, the vastness of space, the incomprehensible scale of creation. It should make us feel incredibly small, incredibly humble.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - "But most of the people do not know." They don't understand that the greatness of the skies is meant to humble them, to remove arrogance from their hearts. They see the sky but learn nothing from it.

translation: And not equal are the blind and the seeing, nor are those who believe and do righteous deeds and the evildoer. Little do you remember
commentary:
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَلَا ٱلْمُسِىٓءُ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ

وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ - "And not equal are the blind and the seeing." You can look at the sky and be blind to its lesson, or you can see it and understand what it teaches about humility.

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَلَا ٱلْمُسِىٓءُ - "Nor are those who believe and do righteous deeds and the evildoer." There's no equivalence between the one who believes and acts righteously and the one who spreads corruption.

قَلِيلًا مَّا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ - "Little do you remember." How little effort you make to truly reflect and remember these truths.

translation: Indeed, the Hour is coming - no doubt about it - but most of the people do not believe
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَـَٔاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا - "Indeed, the Hour is coming - no doubt about it." The Hour is certain, inevitable, approaching.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - "But most of the people do not believe." Despite all the evidence, despite the clear signs, most people refuse to prepare for what's coming.

translation: And your Lord says, "Call upon Me; I will respond to you." Indeed, those who disdain My worship will enter Hell [rendered] contemptible
commentary:
وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ٱدْعُونِىٓ أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِى سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ

وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ٱدْعُونِىٓ أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ - "And your Lord says, 'Call upon Me; I will respond to you.'" This is one of the most beautiful invitations in the Quran. Allah ﷻ Himself is asking us to call on Him, promising He will answer.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِى سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ - "Indeed, those who disdain My worship will enter Hell [rendered] contemptible." Making du'a is called worship ('ibadah) here. Those too arrogant to call on Allah ﷻ will enter Hellfire in a state of humiliation (دَاخِرِينَ). The very arrogance that made them feel too proud to worship will be crushed into utter disgrace.

translation: It is Allah who made for you the night that you may rest therein and the day giving sight. Indeed, Allah is full of bounty to the people, but most of the people are not grateful
commentary:
