ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ - "It is Allah who made for you the night that you may rest therein." The word لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ means to find peace, rest, tranquility. Allah ﷻ designed the night specifically as a time for you to find peace.

This connects beautifully with other ayahs. In Surah Ar-Rum, we learn that spouses are made so you can find peace toward them (لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ إِلَيْهَا). So the place of peace is the spouse, and the time of peace is the night. Where do you find peace? With your spouse. When do you find peace? At night.

The Prophet ﷺ himself found his moments of tranquility (sakinah) at night in prayer. His trouble came during the day when he had to deal with the people and their opposition.

وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا - "And the day bright." He made the day clear so it's easy for you to see and navigate.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ - "Indeed, Allah is full of bounty to the people." He possesses and continuously showers great favors upon humanity.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ - "But most of the people are not grateful." Despite all these blessings, most refuse to be thankful.

translation: That is Allah, your Lord, Creator of all things; there is no deity except Him, so how are you deluded
commentary:
ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ

Now we're returning to the core message of the surah: ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ - "That is Allah, your Lord." After all these signs, all this evidence, this is who your Master is.

خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ - "Creator of all things." Everything in existence comes from Him.

لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - "There is no deity except Him." This is the ultimate truth, the foundation of all faith.

فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ - "So how are you deluded?" Given all this clarity, all this evidence, how in the world are you turned away from the truth?

translation: Thus were those [before you] deluded who were rejecting the signs of Allah
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ يُؤْفَكُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ

كَذَٰلِكَ يُؤْفَكُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ - "Thus were those deluded who were rejecting the signs of Allah." This is the pattern throughout history. Just as people in the past turned away from clear truth, so too do people today. The word يَجْحَدُونَ means to deny something while knowing it's true - to argue against it even when you have no real proof.

translation: It is Allah who made for you the earth a place of settlement and the sky a ceiling and formed you and perfected your forms and provided you with good things. That is Allah, your Lord; then blessed is Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا - "It is Allah who made for you the earth a place of settlement." The word قَرَارًا means a place where you can settle, rest, remain stable.

وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً - "And the sky a canopy." A ceiling, a roof, a structure built over you for protection.

وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ - "And formed you and perfected your forms." He didn't just create you; He sculpted you, fashioned you, and excelled in how He designed you. The word صَوَّرَ means to mold and sculpt something carefully.

وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ - "And provided you from the good things." Sometimes when you design something beautiful, you spend your entire budget on aesthetics and have nothing left for functionality. But Allah's ﷻ budget is limitless. He not only fashioned you perfectly but then provided you with all kinds of good and pure sustenance.

ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ - "That is Allah, your Lord."

فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "So blessed is Allah, Lord of the worlds." The word تَبَارَكَ comes from بَرَكَة - Allah ﷻ is full of the power to increase goodness, to bring out excellence, to multiply blessings. He has the ability to bring out the best in you, to elevate you, to shower you with continuous favor.

translation: He is the Ever-Living; there is no deity except Him, so call upon Him, [being] sincere to Him in religion. [All] praise is [due] to Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۗ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ - "He is the Ever-Living." This divine name الحَيّ emphasizes that while everything else dies, changes, or fades, Allah ﷻ is eternally, perfectly alive.

لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - "There is no deity except Him."

فَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ - "So call upon Him, [being] sincere to Him in religion." This echoes the previous ayah where Allah ﷻ invited us to call on Him. Now He specifies: call on Him with complete sincerity (مُخْلِصِينَ), cutting out every other motive, every other attachment, making your entire way of life (الدِّين) purely for Him.

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "All praise is [due] to Allah, Lord of the worlds."

This final phrase is significant. Allah ﷻ tells us to call on Him with sincerity, and immediately follows it with "Alhamdulillah Rabbil 'Alamin." Every believer who truly reflects on this ayah understands something: every time I stand in prayer and say "Alhamdulillah Rabbil 'Alamin," I'm answering this call. I'm responding to Allah's ﷻ invitation to worship Him sincerely.

And because of this ayah, every time a believer stands in salah and says "Alhamdulillah Rabbil 'Alamin," they should think: "I have to make my life sincere. This isn't just words I'm reciting. This is my commitment to transform how I live, to cut out everything that competes with my devotion to Allah ﷻ."

That one phrase - Alhamdulillah Rabbil 'Alamin - becomes the call to change your entire life.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "Indeed, I have been forbidden to worship those you call upon besides Allah once the clear proofs have come to me from my Lord, and I have been commanded to submit to the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
۞ قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَمَّا جَآءَنِىَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ مِن رَّبِّى وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

This ayah presents the Messenger ﷺ responding to the demands of the disbelievers through a threefold declaration. First, they attempted to intimidate him into worship. Second, they commanded him to worship their false gods. Third, Allah ﷻ instructs him to declare his position with clarity: "I have, in fact, been forbidden from worshiping those that you call on besides Allah ﷻ."

This is not a battle of ideas where one philosophical position can be exchanged for another based on the strength of an argument. The difference between prophets and philosophers is profound. Philosophers can change their philosophy, engage in debate, and shift their positions based on new arguments. But prophets are sanctioned by Allah ﷻ. They have had direct exposure to the angel, to revelation itself. They don't operate from a point of view that says, "I find your argument convincing, so perhaps I'll consider these other gods or alternative theologies."

The ayah continues: لَمَّا جَآءَنِىَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ مِن رَّبِّى - "when the clearest proofs have come to me from my Master." How could the Messenger ﷺ call upon anyone other than Allah ﷻ when the most compelling evidence has reached him directly from his Lord? The statement وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "and I have been commanded to submit myself to the Master of all nations and all peoples" - introduces something unique to Islam.

Consider how theological belief transforms one's entire life in Islam. There is no other system where believing in a set of unseen realities - Allah ﷻ, the angels, the Day of Judgment - begins to affect every single aspect of your physical existence. It impacts your diet, your clothing, the way you speak with family members, your entire outlook on life. This comprehensive transformation is embedded in the word أُسْلِمَ (I submit).

Compare this to other major shifts in world history. The French Revolution changed political structures and certain religious worldviews, but for the most part, people's daily lives remained largely unchanged. What they ate before and after, what they loved and hated, how they entertained themselves - these aspects of life didn't fundamentally transform. The same pattern occurred with the Communist revolution. Culture, tradition, language, dietary habits, family norms - all remained where they were, even as an economic scheme replaced the previous regime.

But when the Prophet ﷺ brought Islam to people, especially after Makkah was conquered, everything changed. The economy of Makkah transformed. The political structure shifted. The sociology of Makkah was revolutionized - how people interacted, the dynamics between men and women, the elimination of class barriers, the transformation of slavery as it existed before. Yet the change went beyond the political, social, and economic spheres. It touched the emotional and spiritual dimensions. What people consumed, their dietary practices, the schedule of their days, when they slept and woke, what they loved and what they hated - everything was transformed.

This kind of change remains unrecorded in human history at any other time or place. From the most individual level all the way to the highest collective level of society, no one has ever brought the kind of comprehensive submission to a worldview that Islam demands and delivers. Other prophets certainly brought this message, but they were not given the victory that the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was given in such a complete, holistic, and lasting way.

translation: It is He who created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then from a clinging clot; then He brings you out as a child; then [He develops you] that you reach your [time of] maturity, then [further] that you become elders. And among you is he who is taken in death before [that], so that you reach a specified term; and perhaps you will use reason
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ ثُمَّ لِتَكُونُوا۟ شُيُوخًا ۚ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَجَلًا مُّسَمًّى وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

He is the One who created you from dust, then from a fluid, then from a clinging substance - عَلَقَةٍ, something that hangs and clings, like the embryo that hangs within and clings to the uterus. Then He brings you out as an infant, then guides you to reach your mature age, your strength, and eventually to become elderly.

The progression described here is deliberate and comprehensive. وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ مِن قَبْلُ - "and some of you die before that happens," before reaching old age. وَلِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَجَلًا مُّسَمًّى - "but regardless, all of you are going to reach that fixed deadline," which is death. Death will come to every single one of you.

The ayah concludes with وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ - "so that you can apply your intellect." As you pass through all these phases of life, you must ask yourself: Is this it? Am I just going to die and turn to dust, and that's the end? Was this incredible creation fashioned with no purpose beyond its temporary existence? Use your intellect to contemplate these stages and recognize that there must be more.

translation: He it is who gives life and causes death; and when He decrees a matter, He but says to it, "Be," and it is
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ فَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

He is the One who gives life and gives death. فَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا - "Then whenever He decides a matter," فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ - "all He does is say to it 'Be,' and it comes into existence."

This statement establishes Allah's absolute authority over existence itself. Life and death are not random occurrences but divine decisions executed with perfect precision and immediacy.

translation: Do you not consider those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah - how are they averted
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ أَنَّىٰ يُصْرَفُونَ

"Have you looked toward those who debate about the revelations of Allah ﷻ? How are they turned away?" أَنَّىٰ يُصْرَفُونَ - they argue and debate about the miracles, the signs, the verses of Allah ﷻ, yet they remain turned away from the truth despite all the evidence before them.

translation: Those who deny the Book and that with which We sent Our messengers - they are going to know
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَبِمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا بِهِۦ رُسُلَنَا ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ

These are الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ - "those who lied against the Book, or those who called the Book a lie." This phrasing carries a significant implication. To call something a lie, you must first be genuinely introduced to it. You cannot reject what you've never properly encountered.

The ayah continues: وَبِمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا بِهِۦ رُسُلَنَا - "and whatever We have sent Our messengers with." Allah ﷻ sent more than just the Book; He also sent a messenger. The disbelievers lied against both.

Consider the difference in how revelation is received. Today, when we tell someone about Islam, we might hand them a copy of the Quran or direct them to read excerpts online. They may read some passages and say, "I'm not impressed," or "I don't see how this is different from any other religion." That is a completely different exposure to the Book than what the Prophet ﷺ provided.

The revelation during the Prophet's time ﷺ was presented in a language completely understood by his audience. It was delivered by someone whose moral character everyone agreed upon before he even opened his mouth. He spoke from a position of unquestioned integrity. We don't have that same position of automatic credibility today.

The ideas were also presented in their proper context, in the original Arabic language. A Quran translation, read simply as a book, may not convey the context in which something was revealed, so the reader may miss its applicability. Much beauty and subtlety is inevitably lost in translation.

Our responsibility becomes clear: we must present a living Quran to people. The Quran must be discussed in discourse with our non-Muslim friends and neighbors. Yet even among Muslims, many don't truly know the Book. How can we share it with others if we haven't internalized it ourselves?

The ayah concludes with a warning: فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ - "Soon they're going to find out." Those who rejected both the Book and the messengers will soon discover the reality of their denial.

## Ayahs 71-72

إِذِ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ وَٱلسَّلَـٰسِلُ يُسْحَبُونَ
فِى ٱلْحَمِيمِ ثُمَّ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ

These ayahs describe the punishment awaiting those criminals discussed earlier. إِذِ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ - "When chains are around their necks," وَٱلسَّلَـٰسِلُ - "and shackles." The word السَّلَاسِلُ comes from the root that suggests something continuous, endless. These are long, unbroken chains that keep extending.

يُسْحَبُونَ - "They will be dragged." The verb comes from the same root as سَحَاب, clouds that seem to drag along slowly. They will be dragged on their faces in humiliation.

فِى ٱلْحَمِيمِ - "In boiling water first," ثُمَّ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ - "then in the Fire they will be put on flames." The punishment comes in stages, each more severe than the last.

## Ayahs 73-74

ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا بَل لَّمْ نَكُن نَّدْعُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Then it will be said to them: "Where are the things you used to associate with Allah ﷻ? Where did they all go?" قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا - "They said: They're lost from us. We can't find them."

Then comes an even more devastating admission: بَل لَّمْ نَكُن نَّدْعُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ شَيْـًٔا - "No, we weren't calling on anything before. We can't even remember them." This represents an added layer of humiliation. Previously, they couldn't stop talking about their false gods. Now they can't even recall their names.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ - "And that is how Allah ﷻ misleads the disbelievers." Their own choices led them to this state of complete confusion and loss.

translation: When the shackles are around their necks and the chains; they will be dragged
In boiling water; then in the Fire they will be filled [with flame]
Then it will be said to them, "Where is that which you used to associate [with Him in worship]
Other than Allah?" They will say, "They have departed from us; rather, we did not used to invoke previously anything." Thus does Allah put astray the disbelievers
[The angels will say], "That was because you used to exult upon the earth without right and you used to behave insolently
commentary:
ذَٰلِكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَمْرَحُونَ

ذَٰلِكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ - "That is because you used to party in the earth without having any right to do so."

The word فَرِحَ means to be overjoyed, happy without justification. Sometimes a person can be overwhelmed with joy, but it must be justifiable. The Quran uses this word in both acceptable and unacceptable contexts. Here, it describes people who enjoyed the world without a care, who showed no concern about injustice, no concern about the afterlife, no thought about their own purpose and existence. They simply partied in the land without any right to do so.

وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَمْرَحُونَ - "And because you went even further in your overjoyed state." مَرَح is the hyperbolic form of فَرَح, suggesting they went to extremes. As time passed, they didn't mature out of this heedlessness. They went even further into it, like a completely hedonistic society where pleasure becomes the ultimate goal. Someone who lives with marah dedicates their entire existence to having a good time.

The word also carries connotations of pride. Allah ﷻ says elsewhere, وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا - "Don't walk on the earth full of pride." First, your ultimate concern becomes pleasure in this world. Then, on top of that, you're filled with arrogance about it.

translation: Enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein, and wretched is the residence of the arrogant
commentary:
ٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ

"Enter the gates of Hell, in which you will remain eternally." فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ - "What a horrible final place for those who are filled with arrogance."

translation: So be patient, [O Muhammad]; indeed, the promise of Allah is truth. And whether We show you some of what We have promised them or We take you in death, it is to Us they will be returned
commentary:
فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۚ فَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ

After describing the fate of the disbelievers, Allah ﷻ turns to console His Messenger: فَٱصْبِرْ - "You remain patient." إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ - "No doubt the promise of Allah ﷻ is true."

فَإِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ - "Maybe it might be the case that We will show you some of the things We've promised them." Some scholars say this happened during Al-Isra wal-Mi'raj, when the Prophet ﷺ was shown glimpses of Paradise, Hellfire, and the Day of Judgment.

أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ - "Or that We would take you away" - meaning, even if you pass away before seeing these promises fulfilled in this world, فَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ - "to Me alone they must return."

This carries a powerful message for the Messenger ﷺ. Allah ﷻ is saying: Remember that they are dealing with Me, not with you. You are simply My messenger. You're not in charge of them. Your job is to deliver the message with patience.

A messenger's job is extraordinarily difficult. He must remain patient while people say things without realizing the magnitude of trouble they're bringing upon themselves. He worries over them, feels stressed about their fate, and begins to blame himself: "Maybe I'm not doing enough." Allah ﷻ addresses this anxiety by reminding him: Even if you were taken away, they would still have to come back to Me. They're not committing an offense against you; they're committing an offense against Me.

Elsewhere in the Quran, Allah ﷻ says, فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ - "They're not calling you a liar; they're denying My revelations" (6:33). This is the reality. You didn't invent this revelation. You shouldn't be personally offended in the same way I am offended.

Consider this analogy: If I write an essay and someone insults it, I'm offended because they're insulting my work. But when the Quran is being insulted, the Messenger ﷺ is being told: You shouldn't be as offended as I am. These are My words, My work. You're simply there to deliver them. So remain patient.

translation: And We have already sent messengers before you. Among them are those [whose stories] We have related to you, and among them are those [whose stories] We have not related to you. And it was not for any messenger to bring a sign [or verse] except by permission of Allah. So when the command of Allah comes, it will be concluded in truth, and the falsifiers will thereupon lose [all]
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قُضِىَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ - "We have sent many messengers way before you." مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ - "Some of them are ones We've told you about," وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ - "and there are so many We didn't tell you anything about."

This reveals that the prophets and messengers mentioned in the Quran represent only a portion of all those Allah ﷻ sent throughout human history. There were countless others whose stories we don't know.

وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ - "It's not becoming of any messenger at all, throughout the ages and even now, to bring a miracle except by Allah's permission."

People would challenge the Prophet ﷺ: "You're really a messenger? Show me something right now. Let's see it." They'd reference previous prophets: لَوْلَا أُوتِيَ مِثْلَ مَا أُوتِيَ مُوسَىٰ - "How come he's not given what Musa (AS) was given?" (28:48). Where's his staff that turns into a snake? Let's see a bush that doesn't burn even though fire is in it. Let's see his hand turn white. Show us these miracles.

Allah ﷻ responds: It's not within any messenger's authority to display a miracle except by Allah's permission. The miracle isn't something the prophet controls. It's not, "Hey, look what I can do." It's given to him to show, and the timing isn't up to him. People cannot place an order for miracles.

The Prophet ﷺ himself acknowledged this. He was delivering the message not because he wanted to, but because he had been charged with it. The miracles Allah ﷻ furnished him with weren't his to display on demand. Allah ﷻ decides when to show what.

فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قُضِىَ بِٱلْحَقِّ - "When the decision of Allah ﷻ comes, it will be decided with truth, with purpose."

This revisits a recurring conversation in the Quran. When people request miracles from prophets, Allah ﷻ consistently references previous nations that witnessed extraordinary signs - the sea parting, the she-camel that drank entire wells, the fire that didn't burn Ibrahim (AS). Yet despite these miracles, they remained مُبْطِلُونَ - they still didn't truly learn or believe.

Allah ﷻ asks: How did those miracles help the nations that came before? That form of miracle didn't secure lasting faith. Over and over again, people witnessed the impossible, only to eventually return to disbelief.

Humanity has now matured enough that instead of physical miracles to witness with their eyes, they're given a compelling message to believe in. They must think about this message and apply their reason, just as they do with all other serious matters in life.

Consider a simple example: You're driving and the radio announces that three miles ahead there's a massive accident blocking traffic. You don't say, "Well, I didn't see it with my own eyes, so I'm just going to keep going." Human beings, when they reach a certain level of maturity, can distinguish between news coming from a reliable source and fabricated information. You assess: Is this information from a trustworthy source? Does it make sense? Does it appeal to your inner conscience? If so, you don't need to see it with your own eyes. That evidence should be sufficient.

The proofs and evidence Allah ﷻ provides demonstrate that He doesn't want us to become Muslim with our eyes closed. Earlier in Surah Yusuf, Allah ﷻ said, قُلْ هَـٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ - "This is my way: I call to Allah ﷻ with insight, with clear evidence, with eyes open" (12:108). Not blind faith - literally faith with eyes wide open. This means we must furnish evidence and proof. We must engage in thoughtful conversation about the validity of our revelation.

We must understand at least some basics to be able to discuss this with others. However, understand one crucial prerequisite: For someone to truly accept this faith, they must maintain the very goodness that Allah ﷻ placed inside them before they even emerged from their mother's womb - the fitrah, the natural decency. If that inner goodness is completely gone, no amount of philosophical arguments will help. But if something of that fitrah remains, this message will appeal to them because it speaks to something deep within their conscience, something they were born with.

In the modern world, the first thing many human beings have denied is the soul itself. "It doesn't exist. I can't see it in a scan. During heart surgery, you don't find a soul inside - just flesh pumping blood." But there is an unseen dimension to the human being that cannot be reduced to the physical realm. If that part of ourselves is denied and rejected, then no amount of evidence will penetrate. If a miracle were to come now and people still refused to believe, that would mean their judgment is sealed - they would be finished.

وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ - "And then and there, those people who try to cancel out this message will have utterly lost."

## Ayahs 79-80

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمَ لِتَرْكَبُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ
وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَلِتَبْلُغُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِى صُدُورِكُمْ وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ

After discussing miracles and revelation, Allah ﷻ redirects attention to the signs already present all around us. Instead of seeking spectacular supernatural events, look at the cattle around you - creatures you ride upon, creatures you eat from.

وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ - "And you have so many benefits from these creatures Allah ﷻ has made and put to your service." وَلِتَبْلُغُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِى صُدُورِكُمْ - "So that you can reach, by putting upon them, the needs that you have deep inside your hearts."

Think about how many of your daily needs are fulfilled by livestock. You want ice cream? It came from a cow. You want soup? The ingredients came from livestock. Your clothing - consider how much leather we use in our lives. The needs we have, even our fundamental requirements, are fulfilled in countless ways by these animals that Allah ﷻ has subjected to our service.

وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ - "And upon them and upon ships you are transported." This connection between land animals and sea vessels emphasizes how Allah ﷻ has facilitated movement and travel through multiple means, each suited to its environment.

These ayahs invite us to recognize that the greatest miracles aren't necessarily dramatic supernatural interventions. Rather, they're embedded in the natural world around us - in the animals we depend on, in the systems that sustain us, in the balance and order of creation itself. The person seeking signs from Allah ﷻ need only open their eyes to the world He has already created.

translation: It is Allah who made for you the grazing animals upon which you ride, and some of them you eat
And for you therein are [other] benefits and that you may realize upon them a need which is in your breasts; and upon them and upon ships you are carried
commentary:
**وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَلِتَبْلُغُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِى صُدُورِكُمْ وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ**

Allah ﷻ continues His discussion of the bounties He has given humanity through livestock, expanding on their comprehensive benefits. The phrase وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ (and for you in them are benefits) encompasses far more than we might initially consider. When you walk through the dairy section of a grocery store, you're witnessing just one dimension of these manāfi' - the milk, cheese, yogurt and countless other products that come from these animals.

But the ayah goes deeper: وَلِتَبْلُغُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا حَاجَةً فِى صُدُورِكُمْ - "and so that you may fulfill through them needs that are in your hearts." This isn't just about physical transportation or material benefit. The word حَاجَةً (need) combined with فِى صُدُورِكُمْ (in your chests/hearts) points to deeply felt human needs - the things you long for, the destinations you dream of reaching, the aspirations that live within you.

Then Allah ﷻ presents a remarkable pairing: وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ - "And upon them and upon ships you are carried." Notice the deliberate contrast here. Livestock represents village life, rural settings, the foundational elements of civilization. Ships represent advanced civilizations, ports, centers of trade and power. Allah ﷻ is showing us that His provisions aren't just for the primitive or the rural. From the simplest village to the most sophisticated civilization, from riding on cattle to sailing on ships or flying in planes, the ability to traverse distances and fulfill your needs comes from Him. The message is universal: whether you live on a farm or in a metropolis, these are all signs from your Creator.

translation: And He shows you His signs. So which of the signs of Allah do you deny
commentary:
**وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ فَأَىَّ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُنكِرُونَ**

The ayah shifts to a pointed question: وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ - "And He shows you His signs." This isn't a one-time showing. The verb form indicates continuous action - He keeps showing you, again and again. The signs are everywhere, constantly before your eyes.

Then comes the challenge: فَأَىَّ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُنكِرُونَ - "So which of the signs of Allah will you deny?" After being surrounded by countless miracles - the animals you ride, the ships you board, the provisions that sustain you - which of these will you continue to reject? The question isn't really asking for an answer. It's exposing the absurdity of denial when evidence is overwhelming. What more miracles are you asking for? How many signs do you need before you acknowledge the One who created all of this?

translation: Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those before them? They were more numerous than themselves and greater in strength and in impression on the land, but they were not availed by what they used to earn
commentary:
**أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ قُوَّةً وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ**

Now Allah ﷻ directs attention to the lessons of history: أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ - "Have they not traveled through the land and observed?" This is an invitation to look with your own eyes, to travel and see for yourself what happened to previous nations.

كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ - "How was the end of those who were before them?" The word عَـٰقِبَةُ (outcome/consequence) emphasizes that every nation's story has an ending, and that ending reveals the truth of how they lived.

Then comes a humbling comparison. Those previous nations - كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِنْهُمْ - they were far greater in number. وَأَشَدَّ قُوَّةً - They were far more powerful, far more intense in their strength. وَءَاثَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - And they left behind mighty ruins and remnants in the earth. Think of the pyramids, the archaeological remains of ancient civilizations, the ruins of the people of Prophet Hud (AS) and Prophet Salih (AS). These were nations that seemed invincible in their time.

Yet the ayah concludes with a devastating reality: فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ - "And all that they used to earn was of no benefit to them whatsoever." Their wealth, their achievements, their sophisticated civilizations - none of it could make them independent from Allah's punishment when it came. Nothing they acquired could protect them or come to their aid.

translation: And when their messengers came to them with clear proofs, they [merely] rejoiced in what they had of knowledge, but they were enveloped by what they used to ridicule
commentary:
**فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَا عِندَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ**

This ayah reveals a pattern that has repeated throughout history - a particular attitude that leads nations to destruction. فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ - "When their messengers came to them with clear proofs" - notice the plural. This happened with messenger after messenger, nation after nation.

What was their response? فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَا عِندَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ - "They rejoiced in what they had of knowledge." This is the attitude of the know-it-all, the intellectual arrogance that has plagued humanity across ages. When clear guidance came to them, they were so overwhelmingly pleased with their own limited knowledge that they dismissed the message entirely.

This is the mindset that says, "Religion is for the feeble-minded, for people who need an escape, who need to believe in something they can't even see. I am far too sophisticated for that. My knowledge, my science, my understanding - this is what matters." They look at revelation with condescension: "This is beneath me. There is nothing here compared to the sophisticated knowledge I already possess."

The irony is devastating. The very pride they took in their knowledge blinded them to真 truth. وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ - "And they were enveloped by what they used to ridicule." The things they mocked - belief in God, the Hereafter, Hellfire, accountability - these very things came to surround them and seize them. They would joke: "Yeah, sure, fire is going to burn me forever but not kill me? That sounds really weird." But their mockery didn't change the reality.

This connects to what Allah ﷻ tells the believers about the Quran: فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا۟ - "In that let them rejoice" (10:58). We are commanded to take joy and pride in the Quran. The contrast is stark: the disbelievers rejoice in their limited human knowledge, while believers should rejoice in divine revelation.

translation: And when they saw Our punishment, they said," We believe in Allah alone and disbelieve in that which we used to associate with Him
commentary:
**فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥ وَكَفَرْنَا بِمَا كُنَّا بِهِۦ مُشْرِكِينَ**

When the inevitable finally arrives, the response is predictable but futile. فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا - "Then when they saw Our punishment" - when they witnessed with their own eyes the war, the destruction, the divine punishment they had denied.

قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥ - "They said: We believe in Allah alone!" Notice the emphasis: وَحْدَهُۥ - alone, by Himself, with no partners. وَكَفَرْنَا بِمَا كُنَّا بِهِۦ مُشْرِكِينَ - "And we disbelieve in what we used to associate with Him." All those false gods, those ideologies, those things they elevated alongside Allah ﷻ - suddenly they're ready to abandon it all.

But this is the declaration of the desperate, not the sincere. When your eyes see the punishment manifesting, when there's no escape, faith at that moment cannot benefit you. The time for belief was when it required courage, when it meant going against the grain, when you had to rely on the unseen. Not when the unseen becomes seen.

translation: But never did their faith benefit them once they saw Our punishment. [It is] the established way of Allah which has preceded among His servants. And the disbelievers thereupon lost [all]
commentary:
**فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنفَعُهُمْ إِيمَـٰنُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا ۖ سُنَّتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ فِى عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ**

The ayah delivers the final verdict with devastating clarity: فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنفَعُهُمْ إِيمَـٰنُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا۟ بَأْسَنَا - "Then their faith could not benefit them at all when they saw Our punishment."

This is the point of no return. Allah ﷻ has shown you miracle after miracle - the signs in creation, the warnings in history, the messages brought by prophets. If the only miracle left for you to see is the Day of Judgment itself, when your physical eyes witness what you denied, then saying "I have faith now" carries no weight whatsoever.

سُنَّتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ فِى عِبَادِهِۦ - "This is the established way of Allah that has already occurred among His slaves." The word سُنَّتَ here refers to Allah's consistent pattern, His established law in dealing with humanity. This isn't arbitrary. It's how He has always dealt with those who reject Him until the moment of their destruction. It's a legacy that has passed, a pattern proven throughout history.

وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ - "And the disbelievers lost right then and there." The word خَسِرَ captures total loss - not just missing out on something, but being placed in ultimate, comprehensive loss. Everything they built, everything they prided themselves on, every moment they spent in denial - all of it culminates in this devastating loss.

---

These ayahs bring Surah Ghafir to a powerful conclusion, connecting the blessings Allah ﷻ has given us with our responsibility to recognize Him. We're living in times when courage is needed - courage to represent Islam properly, to have meaningful conversations about faith with those around us.

Many people today have absolutely no idea what Islam actually teaches. This ignorance isn't just their failure - it's ours. When the Quran constantly shows us prophets and believers engaged in dialogue with aggressive opponents, with people who hate the message, how can Muslims think isolation is the answer?

The Quran itself is primarily a conversation with those who don't believe. If you follow this book from beginning to end, you see constant engagement, constant dialogue, constant invitation. This should inspire every Muslim to want to share their faith - not through forced preaching or ultimatums, but through genuine, heart-to-heart conversation.

These ayahs transition us from the personal dimensions of Tawheed discussed in Surah Az-Zumar and the first part of Surah Ghafir to the next phase: sharing the message. When someone has truly internalized pure devotion to Allah ﷻ, they cannot help but want to share it. The individual believer, having transformed their own life, now takes responsibility to communicate this truth to others. This sets the stage for the surahs to follow, which will explore both individual and collective responsibility in conveying the message of Islam.
